seo.title: Pre-Deploy Evals vs Runtime Checks for AI Agents
seo.description: Compare Pre-Deploy Evaluations and Runtime Checks for AI agent reliability. Understand trade-offs and when to use each approach.

content.intro: Ensuring AI agent reliability requires deliberate strategy at every stage of the development lifecycle. Pre-deploy evaluations and runtime checks represent two complementary philosophies—one focused on catching issues before they reach users, the other on monitoring and correcting behavior as it happens. Understanding the tradeoffs helps teams build more resilient, trustworthy AI systems.

approachA.name: Pre-Deploy Evaluations
approachA.description: Pre-deploy evaluations involve systematically testing AI agents against curated benchmarks, adversarial prompts, and expected use cases before any code ships to production. This approach treats reliability as a gate rather than a continuous process, aiming to surface failure modes in a controlled environment. Teams typically run these evaluations as part of CI/CD pipelines or manual review cycles.
strengths: Catches obvious failure modes and regressions before users are ever affected
Provides a structured, repeatable process that fits naturally into existing engineering workflows
Enables thorough red-teaming and adversarial testing without real-world risk
Creates documented evidence of safety and performance for compliance or audit purposes
weaknesses: Static test sets quickly become stale and fail to reflect the diversity of real-world inputs
Cannot anticipate emergent behaviors that only surface under novel or long-tail conditions
Offers no protection once the agent is live and encountering unexpected user interactions
High-quality evaluation suites are expensive and time-consuming to build and maintain

approachB.name: Runtime Checks
approachB.description: Runtime checks monitor AI agent behavior continuously during live operation, intercepting inputs and outputs to detect anomalies, policy violations, or unexpected reasoning patterns in real time. This approach treats reliability as an ongoing operational concern rather than a one-time pre-launch hurdle. Systems like Vex apply runtime verification to catch issues that evaluations miss and enforce guardrails dynamically.
strengths: Detects and mitigates failures in real time before they cause downstream harm to users or systems
Adapts to the actual distribution of production inputs, including rare and adversarial edge cases
Enables continuous learning by surfacing new failure patterns that can feed back into evaluations
Provides live observability and audit trails for every agent decision and action taken
weaknesses: Adds latency overhead to each agent interaction, which can impact user experience at scale
Requires robust infrastructure and monitoring pipelines that increase operational complexity
Reactive by nature—some harm may occur in the window before a check triggers a response
Defining effective runtime policies requires ongoing tuning as agent behavior and use cases evolve

comparison.approachA: Before deployment only; no coverage once live
comparison.approachB: Continuously during live operation across all interactions

comparison.approachA: Limited to curated test sets that may not reflect production diversity
comparison.approachB: Covers the full spectrum of actual user inputs as they occur
Introduces some overhead per interaction; must be optimized carefully

comparison.approachA: Failures are caught pre-launch but missed issues go undetected until the next eval cycle
comparison.approachB: Issues can be intercepted and mitigated in milliseconds during live use

comparison.approachA: Requires ongoing investment to keep test suites current and comprehensive
comparison.approachB: Requires tuning runtime policies and monitoring infrastructure over time

recommendation.heading: Which Should You Choose?
recommendation.summary: The most resilient AI systems treat pre-deploy evaluations and runtime checks as complementary layers rather than competing alternatives—evaluations establish a quality baseline before launch, while runtime checks provide the continuous safety net that production environments demand. Teams early in their AI journey may start with evaluations alone, but any agent operating at scale or in high-stakes contexts should layer in runtime verification to catch what static tests inevitably miss. The goal is defense in depth: evaluate thoroughly before you ship, then verify continuously once you do.

cta.heading: Try Runtime Verification with Vex
cta.description: See how Vex's runtime verification layer integrates with your existing agents to provide real-time guardrails, live observability, and continuous reliability without slowing down your deployment pipeline.
